policy_statement: Scale parking fees more heavily based on emissions and to promote joint use of vehicles.
mechanism_description: Parking fees will be scaled based on emissions and to promote joint vehicle use, which discourages higher-emitting driving and supports lower transport emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Pricing signal on travel/parking → mode/vehicle/occupancy shift + ↓ vehicle travel → ↓ fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: How people choose to travel and park (vehicle choice, trip frequency, trip length, and vehicle occupancy), by making parking more expensive for higher-emitting vehicles and potentially cheaper for carpools/low-emission vehicles.  
2) Mediating process: Behavioral response to price incentives leads to (a) fewer car trips or shorter trips, (b) higher occupancy (shared rides), and/or (c) substitution toward lower-emission vehicles or public/active transport; this reduces fuel use per passenger-km and total fuel burned.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower tailpipe CO₂ (and other combustion-related GHGs) from reduced gasoline/diesel consumption, yielding net transport emissions reductions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced on-road fuel combustion from decreased high-emitting driving and/or shifts to lower-emitting travel, lowering GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Parking prices differentiated by emissions primarily reduce GHGs by discouraging higher-emitting driving and encouraging shifts to lower-emission modes/vehicles/occupancy, reducing fuel combustion.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it can improve local air quality, reduce congestion, or fund transit (resource efficiency co-benefits), the defining and largest climate-relevant impact of this mechanism is direct mitigation via reduced transport emissions.  
3) Edge cases applied: If pricing is set mainly to manage parking turnover or raise revenue without affecting driving behavior, climate impact could be small; if it strongly incentivizes EV uptake, reductions depend on grid intensity. These do not change the dominant intended pathway for the mechanism class.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any emissions-based parking pricing scheme operates through a cost signal that changes driving/parking choices and thereby reduces (or aims to reduce) transport fuel use and emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether the policy’s main lever is mode shift, VKT reduction, occupancy increases, or fleet electrification requires policy-specific design details and is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Possible rebound/leakage if drivers relocate parking to untolled areas (spillover), or if discounts for EVs increase driving/VKT; net mitigation depends on elasticity, enforcement, and complementary mobility options.
key_indicators: "Parking fees will be scaled"; "from the perspective of emissions"; "promotion of the joint use of vehicles"
co_benefits: Reduced congestion; improved local air quality; reduced noise; potential parking space availability improvements
instance_edge_case_notes: “Scaled more heavily” does not specify the exact tariff structure (e.g., tiers by CO₂ class vs. discounts for carpools), but it is unambiguously a pricing instrument tied to emissions and shared use.
